title: Nap Gulley

infobox.value: (August 29, 1924 – August 21, 1999) was an American [Negro league](/wiki/Negro_league "Negro league") [pitcher](/wiki/Pitcher "Pitcher") in the 1940s.
["Arkansas Baseball Encyclopedia | Napoleon Gulley"](https://arkbaseball.com/tiki-index.php?page=Napoleon+Gulley). *Arkansas Baseball Encyclopedia*. Retrieved 2021-09-13.

**Napoleon Gulley** (August 29, 1924 – August 21, 1999) was an American [Negro league](/wiki/Negro_league "Negro league") [pitcher](/wiki/Pitcher "Pitcher") in the 1940s.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nap_Gulley&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Napoleon Gulley was born on August 29, 1924, in [Huttig, Arkansas](/wiki/Huttig,_Arkansas "Huttig, Arkansas"), to parents Roland G. Gulley and Cinderella Fountain.[[1]](#cite_note-1) He attended [Vashon High School](/wiki/Vashon_High_School "Vashon High School") in [St. Louis, Missouri](/wiki/St._Louis,_Missouri "St. Louis, Missouri"), where he played baseball, basketball, and football.[[2]](#cite_note-Seam-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3)

## Playing career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nap_Gulley&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Playing career")]

### Major Leagues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nap_Gulley&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Major Leagues")]

#### Kansas City Monarchs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nap_Gulley&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Kansas City Monarchs")]

At the age of 16 while playing for local semi pro teams in Missouri, Gulley was signed by [Dizzy Dismukes](/wiki/Dizzy_Dismukes "Dizzy Dismukes"), then general manager of the [Kansas City Monarchs](/wiki/Kansas_City_Monarchs "Kansas City Monarchs"). Gulley briefly joined the Monarchs in spring training before he was traded to the [Birmingham Black Barons](/wiki/Birmingham_Black_Barons "Birmingham Black Barons").[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)

#### Birmingham Black Barons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nap_Gulley&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Birmingham Black Barons")]

Gulley was a member of the Black Barons pitching staff in 1941 and 1942,[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) though he did not make any recorded appearances in league play.

#### Cleveland Buckeyes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nap_Gulley&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Cleveland Buckeyes")]

Gulley joined the [Cleveland Buckeyes](/wiki/Cleveland_Buckeyes "Cleveland Buckeyes") in 1943 and remained on the pitching staff through 1945. Gulley is credited with a single league appearance (a complete game loss) with Cleveland,[[2]](#cite_note-Seam-2) though he often pitched in spring training and exhibitions for the club.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) In 1943, Gulley suffered an unspecified injury and was sidelined by July.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Unhappy with limited playing time in 1945, Gulley asked for and received his unconditional release. Shortly after, he then joined former Black Barons teammate [Ted Radcliffe](/wiki/Ted_Radcliffe "Ted Radcliffe") on Abe Saperstein's Harlem Globetrotters baseball team.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)

#### Chicago American Giants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nap_Gulley&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Chicago American Giants")]

Gulley joined the American Giants late in the 1946 season for a barnstorming tour with the [Chicago Brown Bombers](/wiki/Chicago_Brown_Bombers "Chicago Brown Bombers"), but did not appear in any league games with the club.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)

#### Newark Eagles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nap_Gulley&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Newark Eagles")]

Gulley joined the [Newark Eagles](/wiki/Newark_Eagles "Newark Eagles") in 1947.[[2]](#cite_note-Seam-2) In Newark, Gulley appeared in 3 recorded league contests, losing 2 of them.[[2]](#cite_note-Seam-2)

### Minor Leagues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nap_Gulley&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Minor Leagues")]

#### Seattle Steelheads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nap_Gulley&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Seattle Steelheads")]

Following his release from the Buckeyes, Gulley pitched for the Globetrotters. When the Globetrotters rebranded as the [Seattle Steelheads](/wiki/Seattle_Steelheads "Seattle Steelheads") in the [West Coast Negro Baseball Association](/wiki/West_Coast_Negro_Baseball_Association "West Coast Negro Baseball Association") in 1946, Gulley remained with the club.

#### Barnstorming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nap_Gulley&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Barnstorming")]

After leaving the Steelheads for the Chicago American Giants a month into the 1946 season,[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) he would later reappear with the Cincinnati Crescents, the next evolution of the Steelheads after their league folded. Gulley continued to appear sporadically with numerous teams including the Detroit Senators, Kansas City Royals, and the (rebranded again) Globetrotters.[[9]](#cite_note-9) He also teamed up with other Black major leaguers and prospects in 1948, touring the country with the Jackie Robinson All-Stars.[[10]](#cite_note-10) While barnstorming, Gulley played outfield on days he wasn't pitching.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)

##### Car accident

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nap_Gulley&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Car accident")]

While on the road with the Senators in 1947, Gulley was injured along with 5 teammates when their bus experienced a brake system failure collided with a shed in Covington, Kentucky. Gulley was treated and released at a local hospital for lacerations on his hands and legs.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

#### Mexican League

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nap_Gulley&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Mexican League")]

In 1948, Gulley was recruited with a number of Americans to play in the integrated Mexican League.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) He spent 1948 with the [Alijadores de Tampico](/wiki/Alijadores_de_Tampico "Alijadores de Tampico"). In 1949, Gulley split time between [Tuneros de San Luis Potosi](/wiki/Tuneros_de_San_Luis_Potos%C3%AD "Tuneros de San Luis Potosí") and [Diablos Rojos del Mexico](/wiki/Diablos_Rojos_del_M%C3%A9xico "Diablos Rojos del México").[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12)

#### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nap_Gulley&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Canada")]

Following his time in Mexico, Gulley moved North to the independent [Provincial League](/wiki/Provincial_League_(baseball) "Provincial League (baseball)") in 1949, splitting the season between the [Farmham Pirates](/wiki/Farnham_Pirates "Farnham Pirates") and [St. Jean Braves](/wiki/St._Jean_Braves "St. Jean Braves").[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12)

#### Chicago American Giants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nap_Gulley&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Chicago American Giants")]

in addition to his time in Mexico and Canada, Gulley rejoined the [Chicago American Giants](/wiki/Chicago_American_Giants "Chicago American Giants") in 1949,[[13]](#cite_note-13) now considered a minor league level team.

#### Brooklyn Dodgers organization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nap_Gulley&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Brooklyn Dodgers organization")]

In 1950, Gulley caught the eye of the [Brooklyn Dodgers](/wiki/Brooklyn_Dodgers "Brooklyn Dodgers") and was signed to a minor league contract. He was assigned to the Class C [Santa Barbara Dodgers](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_Dodgers "Santa Barbara Dodgers") of the California League but never played a game with them due to alleged verbal abuse by manager [Bill Hart](/wiki/Bill_Hart_(infielder) "Bill Hart (infielder)"). He then retired for 90 days on the recommendation of minor league president [George Trautman](/wiki/George_Trautman "George Trautman"), making him free to sign with any club he wanted.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)

#### Visalia Cubs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nap_Gulley&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Visalia Cubs")]

Gulley then signed with the [Visalia Cubs](/wiki/Visalia_Cubs "Visalia Cubs"), also in the California League. With Visalia, he transitioned to the outfield full time and hit .292 in his first season.[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12) Gulley became a mainstay and fan favorite and remained with the Cubs through 1952, then rejoining them in 1954.[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12)

#### Victoria Tyees

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nap_Gulley&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Victoria Tyees")]

In 1953, Gulley moved up to the Class A [Victoria Tyees](/wiki/Victoria_Tyees "Victoria Tyees"), hitting .270 in only 28 games.[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12)

#### Spokane Indians

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nap_Gulley&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Spokane Indians")]

In 1955 he joined the Class B [Spokane Indians](/wiki/Spokane_Indians "Spokane Indians") of the Northwest League, hitting .361 and driving in 126 RBI.[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12)

#### Salinas Packers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nap_Gulley&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Salinas Packers")]

Gulley returned to California and played with the [Salinas Packers](/wiki/Salinas_Packers "Salinas Packers"), a [Milwaukee Braves](/wiki/Milwaukee_Braves "Milwaukee Braves") affiliate, in 1956.[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12) During a game against the Fresno Cardinals, Gulley fractured his wrist after he was hit by a pitch.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The injury effectively ended his career in baseball.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)

## Career highlights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nap_Gulley&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Career highlights")]

* In 1946, Gulley pitched a no-hit, no-run game against a team of major and minor league all-stars at San Diego.[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3)
* Gulley led the 1952 California League in doubles with 43.[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12)

## Pitching style

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nap_Gulley&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Pitching style")]

While pitching, Gulley was known for his fastball, though his "out pitch" was an overhand drop curveball.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Life after baseball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nap_Gulley&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Life after baseball")]

After retiring from baseball, Gulley moved to Chicago and opened a graphic arts business.[[16]](#cite_note-16) In 1998, Gulley was honored by the Milwaukee Brewers and added to their Negro League Wall of Fame at County Stadium, later moved to Miller Park.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Death and legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nap_Gulley&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Death and legacy")]

He died in [Skokie, Illinois](/wiki/Skokie,_Illinois "Skokie, Illinois"), in 1999 at age 74. In 2019, Gulley was posthumously elected to the Union County, Arkansas Sports Hall of Fame.[[18]](#cite_note-18)
